career.location: Morristown, St. Lawrence County, New York
career.notes: Bought Arnold Smith hotel fall 1815. Sold to Alfred Hooker Aug 23, 1823.
First collector of port. Overseer of the poor when town organized March 27, 1821.

narrative_blocks.header: St. Lawrence River hotel owner; first port collector
narrative_blocks.body: John Hooker moved his family from Turin to Morristown in the fall of 1815 — a village of only a few houses on the St. Lawrence River — where he bought Arnold Smith's hotel and ran it until 1823. When the town was organized on March 27, 1821, he was appointed overseer of the poor and became its first collector of the port.

narrative_blocks.header: From Connecticut to Turin to the St. Lawrence
narrative_blocks.body: John Hooker left Kensington, Connecticut after his first wife Abigail Goodrich died in 1794, remarried Mary Ives in 1795, and removed the family to Turin, New York in 1802 — then in 1814 to Morristown, beautifully situated on the St. Lawrence River, where he died April 12, 1829.

career.location: Morristown, St. Lawrence County, New York
career.notes: Bought Arnold Smith hotel fall 1815. Sold to Alfred Hooker Aug 23, 1823.
First collector of port. Overseer of the poor when town organized March 27, 1821.

narrative_blocks.header: St. Lawrence River hotel owner; first port collector
narrative_blocks.body: John Hooker moved his family from Turin to Morristown in the fall of 1815 — a village of only a few houses on the St. Lawrence River — where he bought Arnold Smith's hotel and ran it until 1823. When the town was organized on March 27, 1821, he was appointed overseer of the poor and became its first collector of the port.

narrative_blocks.header: From Connecticut to Turin to the St. Lawrence
narrative_blocks.body: John Hooker left Kensington, Connecticut after his first wife Abigail Goodrich died in 1794, remarried Mary Ives in 1795, and removed the family to Turin, New York in 1802 — then in 1814 to Morristown, beautifully situated on the St. Lawrence River, where he died April 12, 1829.

narrative_blocks.header: Child Olive outlived him by seven years
narrative_blocks.body: John Hooker Jr. married Lucy Coffin on November 1, 1815 and died at Morristown December 14, 1825 at thirty-four. His only child Olive survived him by seven years, dying at Morristown July 23, 1832.

narrative_blocks.header: Died at seventeen in Morristown, 1832
narrative_blocks.body: Olive Hooker was born about 1815 in Morristown and died there July 23, 1832 at about seventeen. Her father John Jr. had died in the same village in 1825.

narrative_blocks.header: Both died in South America; son returned alone
narrative_blocks.body: Abigail Hooker married Chester Hoadley in 1812, and in 1838 they traveled to South America on business — taking their eldest child — when both she and Chester died a few months after arriving. Their son returned to the United States alone; the Hoadley genealogy records that the children were born in Alexander, New York.

narrative_blocks.header: The tallest man; died in South America, 1840
narrative_blocks.body: Chester Hoadley measured six feet four inches tall — the Hoadley genealogy calls him "a very tall man" — and traveled to South America on business about 1838 with wife Abigail Hooker and their eldest child. Both Chester and Abigail died there a few months after arriving; their son returned to the United States alone.

narrative_blocks.header: Mother died in South America; father too; returned
narrative_blocks.body: Alexander Hoadley was born about 1838 in Alexander, New York and was taken to South America as an infant when both his parents Chester and Abigail died there a few months after arrival. He returned to the United States but died in early manhood per the Hoadley genealogy.

career.notes: Was in business in San Francisco per Hoadley genealogy.

narrative_blocks.header: San Francisco businessman; parents died in South America
narrative_blocks.body: Frederick Hoadley was born in Alexander, New York and eventually settled in San Francisco as a businessman. The Hoadley genealogy places him there; EH incorrectly identified him as one who went to South America — it was his brother Alexander who made that journey.

career.notes: Appointed Tyler; reappointed Lincoln and Grant.

narrative_blocks.header: Customs collector appointed by Tyler, Lincoln, and Grant
narrative_blocks.body: Henry Hooker served as Collector of Customs at the Port of Morristown, appointed by President Tyler and reappointed by Presidents Lincoln and Grant — spanning decades across the Civil War era. He was also Justice of the Peace, town Supervisor, Episcopal Church Warden, and partner in the mercantile firm Miller, Hooker & Co.

narrative_blocks.header: Son Alfred killed at Monocacy, 1864
narrative_blocks.body: Henry Hooker's son Alfred Ives became Captain of Company B, 106th New York Infantry, and was killed at the Battle of Monocacy on July 9, 1864 — the engagement that slowed Jubal Early's Confederate march on Washington. Alfred died three years before his mother Melinda and eighteen years before his father.

narrative_blocks.header: Husband died at forty-three; she moved to Boston
narrative_blocks.body: Mary Hooker married George Augustus Chapman on December 23, 1845 in Morristown — a merchant doing large Canadian trade. When Chapman died April 5, 1855 at forty-three, Mary moved to Boston with their three children, leaving the St. Lawrence River community where three generations of Hookers had settled.

narrative_blocks.header: Wife died before him; son orphaned at seven
narrative_blocks.body: Horace Henry Hooker married Sarah McAllaster of Boston on September 5, 1855 — but she died December 30, 1865 at thirty-four, leaving their son Henry James just three years old. Horace died July 16, 1869 at forty-one; Henry James was seven years old when orphaned.

narrative_blocks.header: Died at twenty-eight in Morristown
narrative_blocks.body: John Laurens Hooker was born August 17, 1830, married Grace McHugh on February 24, 1857, and died January 4, 1859 at twenty-eight.

military_service.unit: Co. B, 106th New York Infantry Regiment

narrative_blocks.header: Captain killed at the Battle of Monocacy, 1864
narrative_blocks.body: Alfred Ives Hooker was born December 8, 1836 in Morristown and became Captain of Company B, 106th New York Infantry. He was killed July 9, 1864 at the Battle of Monocacy, which delayed Confederate General Jubal Early's march on Washington — later called the battle that saved the capital.

narrative_blocks.header: Left Yale; built Canada's pioneer forwarding business
narrative_blocks.body: Alfred Hooker was educated at Yale but left without graduating to enter commercial life, establishing Hooker & Henderson at Prescott, Ontario — directly across the St. Lawrence from Ogdensburg, New York. The firm grew into Hooker & Hilton and then Hooker & Jaques, known province-wide, until the Grand Trunk Railroad destroyed it.

narrative_blocks.header: Naturalized British; Warden of Prescott's English Church
narrative_blocks.body: Alfred Hooker was naturalized as a British citizen in 1821 and became deeply rooted in Prescott, advancing its schools and religious institutions and serving as Warden of the English Church. He died August 27, 1880; his wife Elvira died two days later in Toronto.

narrative_blocks.header: Moved to Brockville; ten children, most lost
narrative_blocks.body: Mary Ann Hooker married John Clark Potter in 1822 and moved to Brockville, Ontario, where Potter dealt in shoes and leather and became a naturalized British citizen. Of their ten children born at Brockville between 1823 and 1845, six died in infancy or early youth.

narrative_blocks.header: Died at six years old in Morristown, 1816
narrative_blocks.body: Lucretia Hooker was born December 10, 1810 in Turin, New York and died December 26, 1816 at six years old in Morristown on the St. Lawrence. The family had moved there from Turin in 1814, two years before her death.

narrative_blocks.header: Died in Kingston, Canada at fifty-one
narrative_blocks.body: Elijah Hooker was born February 13, 1814 in Turin, New York and died January 29, 1866 in Kingston, Ontario at fifty-one. He was the youngest of John Hooker's seven children by Mary Ives, born the year the family moved to Morristown.

cross_connections.display_label: Susannah Judd's family name connects to the Judd family of.

bio.notes: Widow of Samuel Seymour of Kensington before marrying Elijah Hooker

cross_connections.display_label: His niece's son Abel Hollister came under his guardianship

narrative_blocks.header: Named for a brother who died nine days
narrative_blocks.body: His older brother John was born and died in December 1693 — living only nine days. When the next son arrived in 1695, his parents gave him the same name. He carried it through 71 years, Justice of the Peace and prominent Kensington businessman.

narrative_blocks.header: He and his three sisters all married into
narrative_blocks.body: His sisters Abigail, Mary, and Sarah married three sons of Capt. John Hart between 1719 and 1725. John himself had already married Mercy Hart in 1728 — from a different Hart branch, through Dea. Ebenezer Hart. Four Hooker siblings, two separate Hart lines, one generation.

narrative_blocks.header: Justice of the Peace
narrative_blocks.body: EH describes him as "a prominent and active man in the business of the town." He held the office of Justice of the Peace in Kensington, a parish of Berlin CT, and died there on August 3, 1766, age 71.

parents.father_research_notes: EH says father was Deacon Thomas Hart — WRONG. Barbour Collection Farmington VR confirms father was Dea. Ebenezer Hart (X00119). Primary source wins.

sources.notes: Barbour Collection confirms: father = Dea. Ebenezer Hart

bio.notes: EH gives 'Mercy (Mary) Hart' — both names used interchangeably
